entries.title: Logger overview - a single log of all of Burp's HTTP traffic
entries.body:
# Burp Logger - overview

**Logger** is an end-to-end log of the HTTP traffic of the entire Burp Suite. Its job is to record **every** HTTP request and response that passes through Burp's tools, into a single table in real time. Not only what you ran through the browser, but everything in general: requests from **Scanner**, from **Intruder**, from **Repeater**, from extensions, from session handling rules - any traffic that Burp sends to the server.

## How it differs from Proxy > HTTP history

This is the key point that confuses beginners. At first glance **Logger** and **HTTP history** in **Proxy** look the same - two similar request tables. But they show **different** things:

- **Proxy > HTTP history** shows only the **browser's** traffic that passes through Burp's proxy. If something goes around the proxy (and that is how most of Burp's automation works), it will not be in the proxy history.
- **Logger** shows the traffic of **all** of Burp's tools at once. Including modified requests, extension requests and all the traffic of automatic tools like **Scanner**, which in principle do not go through the proxy.

In other words: **HTTP history** answers the question "what did the browser send?", and **Logger** answers the question "what did Burp send to this server in general?".

## Why Logger is needed

- **See the real traffic of the automation.** When **Scanner** has found something (or, conversely, is behaving strangely), **Logger** shows the exact requests it sent and the responses it received. This is the only built-in way to look "under the hood" of the scanner at the level of individual HTTP messages.
- **Debugging extensions.** Any extension (a BApp or your own) generates traffic while working. If an extension does not do what you expect, **Logger** will show which requests it actually sends, with which headers and where to.
- **Monitoring session handling rules.** If you have configured session handling rules (for example, automatic insertion of a fresh CSRF token or a re-login), **Logger** lets you see the final request, already modified by the rules - what actually went out on the wire, not what you wrote.
- **A full picture of the target.** During an active test several tools hit the server at once. **Logger** brings this whole flow together into one sorted and filterable log, where the overall activity is visible.
- **Finding "lost" requests.** Sometimes you need to find a specific request, but you do not remember which tool you sent it with. It is definitely in **Logger** - it can be found by filter regardless of the source.

## What is inside the tool

**Logger** is not just a passive table. It has:

- a **Tool column**, showing which Burp tool produced each request (Proxy, Scanner, Repeater, Intruder, Extensions, etc.);
- **custom columns** based on scripts (Bambdas) to output arbitrary request/response details right in the table;
- a **Capture filter** - what to record in the log at all (it also controls memory consumption);
- a **View filter** - what part of what is recorded to show on screen;
- a **Task Logger** - a separate, focused log of the traffic of one specific task (for example, a single scan).

## Editions

**Logger** is available in both **Community Edition** and **Professional**. The basic log, viewing and filtering are present in both editions. Some advanced capabilities - custom columns and script-based filtering (Bambdas), as well as filtering by a search term - belong to **Professional**.

## A tip from practice

Keep **Logger** running in the background during an active test, but do not forget about the **Capture filter**: without limits the log swells to hundreds of megabytes on a large scan and eats up memory. A sensible strategy is to limit the capture to scope (in-scope) and discard the obviously uninteresting stuff (static files, fonts, images) at the **Capture filter** stage, so that only what is really worth looking at stays in memory.

entries.title: Getting started with Logger
entries.body:
# Getting started with Logger

This section is a quick start: how to open **Logger**, enable recording, read the log and view an individual request with its response.

## Open Logger and enable recording

1. Go to the **Logger** tab in Burp's main window (it is in the same row as **Proxy**, **Intruder**, **Repeater** and the rest).
2. Recording is controlled by the **Logging: On / Off** toggle at the top. While it is in the **On** position, new requests go into the log; **Off** - recording is paused (what is already recorded stays).
3. Next to it is a **trash icon** - it clears the entire log. The action is irreversible: cleared entries cannot be brought back.

As soon as recording is enabled, start generating traffic - browse the application through the browser, launch a scan or send a couple of requests from **Repeater**. Rows will start appearing in the table in real time.

## What goes into the log

**Logger** shows all the HTTP exchange between Burp's tools and the server. Unlike the proxy history, the traffic of automatic tools (for example, **Scanner**), which does **not** go through the proxy, also gets in here. That is exactly why **Logger** is indispensable when you need to see Burp's full activity against the target, not just what the browser sent.

## Reading the table

Each row is one request (and its response, if there is one). Among the default columns, the most important is **Tool**: it says which Burp tool produced the request. This immediately answers the question "who sent this - the browser, the scanner or an extension?".

Columns are sorted by clicking the header. For example:

- clicking the **#** column orders the entries by arrival order (chronologically);
- clicking **Tool** groups the traffic by tool;
- clicking the status column - by response codes, to quickly pick out, say, all the `500`.

## Viewing a request and response

Select any row - at the bottom the **message editor** opens, showing both the request itself and the server's response to it. The usual display modes (**Pretty / Raw / Hex**) and message search work here. In **Logger** this view is meant for analysis: entries are studied, not edited in place (for editing and resending, the request is sent to **Repeater** or **Intruder**).

## Two filters - the difference right away

Right from the start it is useful to understand that there are **two** filters, and they work differently:

- **Capture filter** - decides which requests to **record** in the log at all. It also controls memory usage: what is discarded at capture does not get into the log and does not occupy memory.
- **View filter** - decides which of the already recorded requests to **show**. Entries hidden by it stay in memory and return to the screen as soon as you change the display conditions.

A simple rule: the **Capture filter** throws data away for good, the **View filter** only temporarily hides it from view.

## Recommended first run

```
1. Open the Logger tab
2. Logging: On
3. Configure the Capture filter: keep only in-scope (so as not to capture extra stuff)
4. Generate some traffic (browser / scan / Repeater)
5. Click a row -> read the request and response in the editor at the bottom
6. View filter - narrow the display to the current task (for example, only Scanner or only 5xx codes)
```

## Tip

If you have just enabled **Logger** during an already running test, do not be surprised by an empty table: the log records traffic **from the moment it is enabled**, it does not pull in past requests retroactively. For what has already been sent, look at the proxy history or the history of the specific tool.

entries.title: Working with Logger entries
entries.body:
# Working with Logger entries

When traffic is already in the log, the main work begins: scrolling through entries, reading requests/responses, marking the interesting ones and sending findings further along the tools. This section is about everyday actions with the table's rows.

## Viewing an entry

Select a row - at the bottom the request and response appear in the **message editor**, plus the **Inspector** is available for conveniently breaking the message into parts (parameters, headers, cookies). In **Logger** these panels work **in view mode**: the message is studied, but not edited in place. This makes sense - the log records what has already happened; if a request needs to be changed and resent, it is sent to **Repeater**.

## Sorting and configuring the table

The **Logger** table is flexibly configurable:

- **sorting** - clicking any column header;
- **rearranging columns** - by dragging the headers;
- **column visibility** - you can hide the unnecessary ones and show the needed ones, keeping only the relevant stuff on screen;
- **copying column data** to the clipboard.

On a large log, sensible sorting saves time: sort by response code to pick out all the server errors, or by response length to spot abnormally large/small responses.

## Annotations: highlighting and comments

So as not to lose interesting entries in the flow, they are marked:

- **Highlighting.** Click the **#** column in the row and choose a color from the drop-down list. Handy for visual separation: red for vulnerability candidates, yellow for "look at later", green for verified.
- **Comment.** Double-click the **Comment** column - write a note. For example: "the q parameter is reflected -> XSS candidate" or "returns 500 on a quote".

Annotations are saved together with the project, so when you return to the test a day later you will not lose the context.

## Sending an entry to other tools

The main value of the log is being an entry point into further analysis. Right-clicking an entry opens a context menu, from which the request can be sent on:

- **Send to Repeater** (**Ctrl+R**) - for manual editing and resending (checking injections, IDOR, logic);
- **Send to Intruder** (**Ctrl+I**) - for automated iteration (payloads, parameter fuzzing);
- **Send to Organizer** - set aside an interesting request in **Organizer** to come back to it later without losing it in the general flow;
- sending to other tools and accompanying actions (copying the URL, copying as a request, etc.).

A typical flow: you noticed a strange response from **Scanner** in **Logger** -> **Send to Repeater** -> reproduced it by hand -> brought it to a reliable PoC.

## Custom columns (Professional)

In **Professional** you can add **your own script-based columns** (Bambdas), which output arbitrary details of the logged items right in the table - for example, the value of a specific response header or the body length. This turns the log into a targeted-search tool: instead of opening each entry by hand, you see the needed indicator right in the column (in detail - in the section on custom columns).

## Export to CSV

Select the entries you need and choose the export item **Export as CSV** in the context menu (right-click). Burp neatly prepares the data for external analysis (including for Excel):

- dates are encoded in the **ISO 8601** format;
- binary data is converted to **Base64**;
- special characters are escaped.

This is handy when you need to feed the export into a script, a spreadsheet, or attach an array of requests to a report.

## Tips

- **Mark things immediately.** Saw something interesting - highlight + comment right away. In a flow of thousands of rows, an unfound entry is as good as a lost one.
- **Sorting by status and length** are the two most useful views of the log: the first picks out errors and redirects, the second - responses that are anomalous in size (leaks, successful injections).
- **Logger is an entry point, not a workbench.** Do the analysis itself in **Repeater**: the log is there to find a request and send it on.

entries.title: Custom columns in Logger
entries.body:
# Custom columns in Logger

The standard columns (method, URL, status, length, **Tool**, etc.) are not always enough. Sometimes you need to see a **specific detail** of a request or response in the table - the value of some header, the presence of a token, the size of a certain part. For this, **Logger** (the **Professional** edition) has **custom columns**, which are computed by a script and output the result right into the table row.

## What it runs on: Bambdas and the Montoya API

A custom column is a short Java script (**Bambda**) that, for each entry, returns a value to display. The script has access to two **Montoya API** objects:

- `LoggerHttpRequestResponse` (in the editor usually as `requestResponse`) - gives access to the request, the response, the service data (host/port), the time of receipt and the timings;
- `Utilities` - Burp's helper functions (encoding/decoding, working with strings, etc.).

The script must return a value that Burp will show in the cell (usually a string).

## How to add a custom column

Open the column settings menu: **Logger > options menu (icon) > Add custom column**. From there, two paths.

**Load a ready script from the library.** In the **Add custom column** window click **Load** - you can choose recent scripts or click **View all** and scroll through your whole **Bambda library** (a personal collection of reusable scripts, including ones you saved or imported from GitHub).

**Write your own.** In the same window:

1. start from an empty definition or choose **New > From template** (a built-in template);
2. enter the name of the future column in the **Column header** field;
3. write/edit the Java code that defines what to show;
4. click **Apply** to compile and check (Burp will show compilation errors - fix them);
5. if you wish, save the script to the library for reuse;
6. click **Apply & close** - the column appears in the table.

## Example: output the Server header from the response

A classic example is to show the value of the `Server` header in a separate column (you can quickly see where there is nginx, where Apache, where something exotic):

```java
if (!requestResponse.hasResponse()) { return ""; }
var response = requestResponse.response();
return response.hasHeader("Server") ? response.headerValue("Server") : "";
```

The logic is simple: if there is no response - an empty cell; if there is one and it contains the `Server` header - we output its value, otherwise also empty.

## Ideas for useful columns when hunting

By analogy with the example it is easy to assemble columns for your own tasks:

- **Presence/value of security headers** - `Content-Security-Policy`, `Strict-Transport-Security`, `X-Frame-Options`: you can immediately see which responses do not have protection configured.
- **Type/value of Set-Cookie** - for example, output whether the `HttpOnly` or `Secure` flag is set.
- **Custom application headers** - some `X-Backend`, `X-Cache`, `X-Request-Id`: helps to map the infrastructure and routing.
- **Length of a specific part** - for example, the response body length as a separate column for quick visual searching of anomalies.
- **A reflection indicator** - whether the response contains a substring from the request (a rough marker of a reflected XSS candidate).

## Why this is needed

The main point of custom columns is to **shift the analysis from "opening each entry by hand" to "seeing the needed indicator right in the table"**. On a log of thousands of rows, a column that highlights, say, the absence of CSP or the presence of a suspicious header saves hours: you sort/scan one column with your eyes instead of opening messages one by one.

## Tips

- **Always return a defined value.** Do not forget the "no response" branch (`hasResponse()`), otherwise the column will behave unpredictably on requests without a response.
- **Keep the script light.** The column is computed for many rows; heavy logic will slow the table down.
- **Save the good columns to the library.** A good column (for example, "no CSP") will come in handy in every following project - do not write it again from scratch.

entries.title: Filtering in Logger: Capture filter and View filter (including Bambda scripts)
entries.body:
# Filtering in Logger: Capture filter and View filter

During an active test the **Logger** log fills with an avalanche of requests. So as not to drown in it (and not to eat up memory), **Logger** has **two different filters**. Understanding the difference between them is critical.

## Two filters - the fundamental difference

- **Capture filter** - controls which items are **recorded** in the log. What is discarded at capture is lost **forever**: even if you later loosen the filter, those requests cannot be brought back. On the other hand, what is discarded does not occupy memory.
- **View filter** - controls which of the already recorded items are **shown** on screen. What is hidden by it stays in **Logger**'s memory and appears again as soon as you change the display conditions.

In short: the **Capture filter is destructive** (it deletes data to save memory), the **View filter is non-destructive** (it only temporarily hides what is already captured). The practical conclusion: at capture, discard only what you **definitely** do not need (static files, fonts), and do the fine-tuning of "what to look at now" through the **View filter**.

## Two configuration modes for both filters

Both the **Capture filter** and the **View filter** are configured in two ways:

- **Settings mode** - checkboxes and drop-down lists (quick and visual);
- **Script mode** - Java scripts (**Bambdas**) for arbitrary filtering logic.

A handy technique: configure the filter with checkboxes in **Settings mode**, then click **Convert to script** at the bottom of the window - Burp will turn your settings into a script that you can then refine by hand. This is the best way to learn to write filters: you see what Burp translated the usual checkboxes into.

## Capture filter - criteria in Settings mode

- **Capture limit** - the memory limit for the log (there are presets and your own value; by default around 50MB/100MB). When the limit is reached - old entries are evicted.
- **Capture by request type** - by request type: **Capture only in-scope items** (only what is in scope), **Discard items without responses** (discard requests without a response), **Capture only parameterized requests** (only requests with parameters).
- **Capture by MIME type** - by the MIME type of the response (HTML, XML, etc.): what to capture and what not.
- **Capture by status code** - by response code: which codes to record in the log.
- **Capture by tool** - by Burp tool: whose traffic to capture (by default - all).
- **Capture by search term** (**Professional** only) - by a search term, with the options **Regex** (literal or regular expression), case sensitivity and **Negative search** (capture what did NOT match).
- **Session handling** - a toggle to discard service session handling requests.
- **Limit request/response size** - a limit on the request/response size (by default up to 1MB).

## View filter - criteria in Settings mode

The set is similar, but the wording is about "display" rather than "capture", plus it has its own items:

- **Filter by request type** - **Show only in-scope items**, **Hide items without responses**, **Show only parameterized requests**.
- **Filter by MIME type** - show only the selected MIME types.
- **Filter by status code** - show only the selected response codes.
- **Filter by tool** - show the traffic of only the selected tools.
- **Filter by search term** (**Professional**) - with the options **Regex**, **Case sensitive**, **Negative search**.
- **Filter by file extension** - by file extension (for example, show only `php`, hide `css`).
- **Filter by annotation** - show only annotated entries (with a comment or highlight).

## Script-based filtering (Bambdas)

When the checkboxes are not enough, switch to **Script mode** and write the logic in Java. The script must return a **boolean**: `true` - let the item through (capture/show), `false` - discard/hide.

The available **Montoya API** objects differ slightly between the two filters:

- the **Capture filter** has `LoggerCaptureHttpRequestResponse` and `Utilities`;
- the **View filter** has `LoggerHttpRequestResponse` and `Utilities`.

**Capture filter example - do not capture fonts:**

```java
var mimeType = requestResponse.mimeType();
return mimeType != MimeType.FONT_WOFF && mimeType != MimeType.FONT_WOFF2;
```

Everything is captured except WOFF/WOFF2 fonts - a typical cleanup of the log from useless static files right at the entrance.

**View filter example - show only slow responses (>1000 ms):**

```java
if (!requestResponse.hasResponse()) {
    return false;
}
var timingData = requestResponse.timingData();
return timingData.timeBetweenRequestSentAndStartOfResponse().toMillis() > 1000;
```

Such a filter keeps on screen only the requests whose response took longer than a second - handy when hunting blind time-based vulnerabilities (time-based SQLi, SSRF with a delay) and when looking for heavy endpoints.

## How to apply a script

1. Open **Logger** and click the filter bar.
2. Choose **Script mode** in the window of the filter you need.
3. Write the script from scratch or take a template / load one from the library.
4. Click **Apply** - compilation and checking; Burp will show errors in the compilation panel, fix them.
5. If you wish, save it: **Save to library**.
6. Click **Apply & close** - the filter is activated (it applies both to the already captured items and to future ones).

## Important note about performance

The **Capture filter** script is run against **all** of Burp's outgoing HTTP messages - inefficient code noticeably slows down the work. So keep capture scripts as light as possible; it is better to move heavy logic into the **View filter**, which works on the already captured set.

## Tip

Do not confuse the purpose of the filters. If you are not sure whether you will need some requests later - **do not cut them off with the Capture filter**. Capture by a broad criterion (at least in-scope), and narrow the view with the non-destructive **View filter**: its conditions can be changed back and forth as much as you like, without losing anything.

entries.title: Task Logger - the traffic log of a single task
entries.body:
# Task Logger

**Task Logger** is a narrowly specialized log that shows the traffic of **one specific task**: for example, an individual scan or crawl. Whereas the main **Logger** brings the traffic of all tools together into one common log, the **Task Logger** isolates and shows only what was sent by **this exact** task - without the noise from the rest of Burp.

## When it is needed

- **Figure out what a scan is doing.** The scanner is behaving unexpectedly - taking too long, sending to the wrong place, producing strange findings. The **Task Logger** shows the exact requests and responses of **this** scan, and you can immediately see what is happening.
- **Track the task's progress.** You can watch in real time which requests the task is sending right now.
- **Debugging without noise.** When several tools are working in parallel, the common **Logger** is overloaded. The **Task Logger** gives a focused view of one operation.

## How to open it

1. Go to the **Dashboard**.
2. In the **Tasks** list, select the task you need (or several).
3. In the main panel on the right, open the **Logger** tab.

This tab will show the log that relates only to the selected task.

## How it differs from the main Logger

It works similarly to the ordinary **Logger** (the same table, viewing of the request/response), but with two important differences:

- **Less memory for the log.** By default only **10MB** is allocated per task, or **20MB** if Burp has access to **1GB or more** of memory. That is, a deep retrospective on a large task is not guaranteed here - the log is meant for quick diagnostics, not for a full archive.
- **No filter by tool.** Capturing and filtering by **Tool** are unavailable - and logically so: the **Task Logger** already shows the traffic of only one tool (the one performing the task). There is simply nothing to filter by source here.

## What it shows

Exclusively the traffic of the selected task: the requests it sent and the responses it received. No outside traffic - exactly the network activity that this operation produced.

## A practical scenario

Suppose **Scanner** reported a finding that you do not believe in, or, conversely, missed an obvious vulnerability. The order of actions:

1. **Dashboard > Tasks** - select this scan.
2. Open the **Logger** tab in the task panel.
3. Look through the scan's real requests: which payloads it substituted, which responses it received, where it stopped.
4. Found a suspicious request - send it to **Repeater** and reproduce the finding by hand, to confirm or refute it.

## Tip

Keep the **10MB/20MB** limit in mind: on a long scan, old entries in the **Task Logger** are evicted, and the task's early requests may already be unavailable. If you need a full picture of the traffic - configure the common **Logger** with a suitable **Capture filter** in advance, before launching the task.
